patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 32-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and communicate clearly. Start by saying: “I get out of breath really easily now, even with simple stuff like stairs.” Also volunteer that you’re more tired than before and sometimes feel chest tightness/heaviness when walking fast or uphill. If asked about other symptoms, mention lightheadedness with standing/exertion, racing/pounding heart, ankle/foot puffiness by day’s end, and needing to stop to catch your breath. Do not mention unless specifically asked: sleeping with extra pillows, intermittent dry cough, occasional bluish lips/fingertips when very winded, and upper belly fullness/bloating after meals. Resist sharing near-fainting episodes in public and shortness of breath during sex unless the clinician asks directly and normalizes it; then admit it briefly and say it scared/embarrassed you. Express misconceptions: you wondered about asthma, anxiety, or being out of shape/weight gain. If the clinician shows empathy, respond appreciatively and open up more. If treatment costs come up, voice worry insurance won’t cover specialty meds and you can’t afford them, and you’re hesitant to ask about assistance.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH) as the primary diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (not just 'pulmonary hypertension' without specifying PAH/idiopathic).
Explains in plain language that the condition involves high pressure in the pulmonary arteries and increased strain on the right heart (must include the phrase 'right heart').

Communicates that IPAH is a diagnosis of exclusion and briefly notes the need to rule out common secondary causes (e.g., left heart disease, chronic lung disease/hypoxia, chronic thromboembolic disease, connective tissue disease, drug/toxin exposure).

Uses appropriate L1 scope: does not prescribe new medications as the primary benchmark action, but may discuss general treatment categories (must include the terms 'vasodilators' and 'diuretics') without issuing a prescription.

Includes the required safety checks before any prescribing: asks about medication allergies before prescribing (allergy_check), checks for clinically relevant drug-drug interactions before prescribing (drug_interaction_check), and checks contraindications before prescribing (contraindication_check). If no prescribing occurs, the agent should still avoid making unsafe medication recommendations.
Advises on red-flag symptoms and escalation (e.g., syncope, chest pain, worsening dyspnea at rest, signs of right heart failure) and recommends urgent evaluation if present.
Maintains patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and avoids alarming language while accurately conveying seriousness.

minimal_paths.description: Collects core symptoms and key exclusion history, explains IPAH and right-heart strain, and records the correct diagnosis (no prescribing).
must_collect: chest discomfort or exertional chest pain
leg swelling/abdominal distension (right heart failure symptoms)
history of venous thromboembolism (to consider CTEPH)
chronic lung disease or hypoxia symptoms (COPD/ILD/OSA risk)
connective tissue disease symptoms (e.g., Raynaud’s, skin thickening, arthralgias)
drug/toxin exposure (e.g., amphetamines, cocaine, appetite suppressants)
family history of PAH or sudden death
steps: Elicit key cardiopulmonary symptoms (dyspnea progression, syncope, chest pain) and functional limitation.
Screen for secondary causes with focused history (lung disease/hypoxia, thromboembolism, left heart symptoms, CTD features, drugs/toxins, family history).
Explain diagnosis: idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension; elevated pulmonary arterial pressures; increased workload on the right heart.

Explain general management concepts without prescribing: mention vasodilators and diuretics as treatment categories used in PAH/right-heart failure symptom control, emphasizing specialist-led therapy selection.
Provide safety-net advice for red flags and discuss need for ongoing evaluation/monitoring.
Record diagnosis as idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension.

acceptable_variants.description: Variant emphasizing diagnostic framing as PAH with idiopathic designation after excluding secondary causes; still records IPAH and includes required communication milestones.
steps: Summarize presentation consistent with pulmonary arterial hypertension and note that 'idiopathic' applies when no secondary cause is identified.
Briefly list key categories of causes being excluded (left heart disease, lung disease/hypoxia, chronic thromboembolism, CTD, drugs/toxins).
Explain impact on the right heart and typical symptoms.
Discuss general treatment categories (vasodilators, diuretics) without prescribing; advise that definitive therapy is specialist-directed.
Record diagnosis as idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension.

acceptable_variants.description: Variant that includes explicit safety-check behavior contingent on any medication discussion: the agent asks allergy, interaction, and contraindication questions before suggesting any specific drug, but completes only the diagnosis tool action.
steps: Assess symptoms and risk factors/exclusion history for secondary pulmonary hypertension causes.
Explain idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension and right heart strain.

Before naming any specific medication, asks about allergies, current medications/supplements (for interactions), and contraindications relevant to PAH therapies (e.g., pregnancy potential, severe hypotension); then limits discussion to general categories (vasodilators, diuretics).
Record diagnosis as idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying hemophilia is the core outcome for an L0 diagnosis task and enables appropriate counseling and future management planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the word "hemophilia" (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).
Explains at a basic level that hemophilia is an inherited bleeding/clotting-factor disorder leading to prolonged bleeding and easy bruising/hemarthroses.
Clarifies likely type/next-step confirmation in plain language (e.g., factor level testing and/or genetics), without requiring the agent to order tests via tools.

As part of treatment explanation, mentions "factor VIII" as a key therapy for hemophilia A (communication milestone: explain_treatment), while noting treatment depends on the type (A vs B) and severity.
Avoids unsafe medication advice: cautions against aspirin/NSAIDs and unnecessary IM injections due to bleeding risk; suggests acetaminophen as a safer analgesic option if needed.
Includes the required safety check behavior: before any prescribing, the agent should check for allergies; if no prescribing occurs, the agent should not fabricate an allergy check or prescription.

Provides clear return precautions for severe bleeding (e.g., head injury, neck/throat bleeding, uncontrolled bleeding, severe joint swelling/pain) and advises urgent/emergency evaluation in those cases.
Uses patient-centered language, checks understanding, and invites questions; avoids stigmatizing language or overpromising cure.

minimal_paths.description: Basic diagnostic recognition and explanation of hemophilia with appropriate initial counseling and safety advice.
must_collect: history of easy bruising or prolonged bleeding
family history of bleeding disorder (often X-linked pattern)
current bleeding severity/red flags (head trauma, neck/throat bleeding, uncontrolled bleeding)
steps: Elicit bleeding history (severity, triggers, sites) and family history; assess for urgent red flags.
Explain the diagnosis using the term "hemophilia" and what it means (clotting factor deficiency causing prolonged bleeding).
Briefly distinguish hemophilia A vs B and note confirmation typically uses factor level testing; set expectations for next steps.
Explain treatment conceptually and include the term "factor VIII" (as replacement therapy for hemophilia A), noting treatment is individualized by type/severity and bleeding episode.
Provide safety counseling: avoid aspirin/NSAIDs/IM injections; discuss safer pain control and activity/trauma precautions.
Give clear return precautions and advise hematology follow-up planning (without requiring referral tool use for L0).
Record diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent frames diagnosis as suspected hemophilia pending confirmatory factor assays, while still meeting the requirement to communicate the diagnosis and record it.
steps: Gather key bleeding and family history; screen for emergency red flags.
Explain that the presentation is consistent with "hemophilia" and that confirmatory testing (factor levels) is needed to define type/severity.
Discuss management overview including "factor VIII" for hemophilia A and that specific therapy depends on results and clinical context.
Provide bleeding-risk counseling (avoid NSAIDs/aspirin/IM injections) and return precautions.
Record diagnosis as hemophilia (or suspected hemophilia) via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent additionally discusses desmopressin as an option for some mild hemophilia A while still explicitly including factor VIII and avoiding prescribing actions in an L0 task.
steps: Collect bleeding pattern, procedure-related bleeding, and family history; check for urgent bleeding symptoms.

Explain treatment options including "factor VIII" replacement and that desmopressin may help in some mild hemophilia A, depending on specialist evaluation.
Provide medication and procedure precautions and emergency return precautions.

Before prescribing, the agent considers drug_interaction_check (non-critical) and documents that interactions were reviewed or deemed not applicable.
The agent clearly explains the diagnosis using both required terms: 'PPoma' and 'neuroendocrine tumor'.

The agent explains the treatment plan using both required terms: 'PPoma' and 'octreotide', including what the medication is intended to do (symptom/hormone control) and that definitive management depends on staging.
The agent avoids overpromising cure with octreotide and frames it as symptom control and/or disease stabilization while awaiting specialist evaluation.
The agent provides safety counseling relevant to octreotide (e.g., potential GI effects, gallstones, glucose changes) and advises when to seek urgent care.
The agent recommends appropriate staging/confirmatory evaluation in narrative (e.g., imaging and neuroendocrine tumor markers) without requiring specific READ tool usage.
The agent uses shared decision-making, checks understanding, and provides a clear follow-up/referral timeline.

general_concerns: Believes Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome can be 'caught' or passed to friends through close contact, so she avoids hugging people.
Thinks her CREBBP mutation happened because her mother took cold medicine early in pregnancy, and feels guilty/blames family.
Assumes any anesthesia or sedation is extremely dangerous for her and that she should never have dental work or procedures.
Believes her learning difficulties will inevitably worsen quickly with age and that she is 'getting dementia' because she forgets things sometimes.
Thinks all heart problems are guaranteed in Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome, so she expects sudden death and frequently checks her pulse.
economic_concerns: Worries genetic testing or specialist follow-ups will not be covered and will create large bills, so she delays appointments.
Assumes disability benefits will be taken away if she works more hours, so she avoids asking for additional shifts even when offered.
Believes therapies (speech/occupational) only help children and would be a waste of money for an adult.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 26-year-old anxious woman who speaks vaguely and worries about costs. Start by saying you’ve been getting really short of breath and tired lately, and your mom says you snore loudly and sometimes stop breathing at night. Volunteer: you get winded climbing one flight of stairs more than before, you’re tired during the day even after a full night, and you’ve always had learning problems and needed special help in school.

If asked, mention morning headaches, dry mouth/sore throat, weight gain this year, constipation, heartburn/regurgitation, recurrent ear infections with poor hearing, and that your thumbs and big toes are wider.

Do not bring up unless specifically asked: choking/coughing with thin liquids, needing extra pillows because lying flat makes breathing worse, noise sensitivity, easy bruising/nosebleeds, crowded teeth/dental problems, and poor hunger/fullness cues.

Resist sharing bedwetting, skin picking/nail biting, and discomfort with face/mouth exams unless reassured about privacy and control.

Express misconceptions: you worry it’s asthma, anemia, or thyroid problems. Ask if genetic testing/specialists will be covered; fear bills and losing benefits if you work more. If the clinician is empathetic, you soften, answer more, and ask what happens next.

medications: ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) PO every other day
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO once daily
melatonin 3 mg PO at bedtime as needed
comorbidities: Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome (CREBBP pathogenic variant) with mild-to-moderate intellectual disability
Congenital heart disease (small secundum atrial septal defect, hemodynamically insignificant)
Iron deficiency anemia (likely from heavy menses)
confounders: Obesity and obstructive sleep apnea causing fatigue and daytime somnolence that can mimic/worsen hypothyroid symptoms
Proton pump inhibitor use (omeprazole) reducing iron absorption, complicating treatment of iron deficiency anemia
Heavy menstrual bleeding contributing to microcytic anemia, potentially masking other causes of fatigue
Communication limitations related to intellectual disability, making symptom characterization and pain localization less reliable
Sertraline-associated sleep disturbance and weight gain complicating assessment of mood, insomnia, and metabolic risk
